mnemonics.title: Euler's Formula
mnemonics.content: Nice Math Functions: n - m + f = 2 (Remember: Nice = n, Math = m, Functions = f)

mnemonics.title: Max Edges in Planar Graph
mnemonics.content: For simple planar: 3 Friends (3n-6), For bipartite: 2 Friends (2n-4)

mnemonics.title: Kuratowski's Non-Planar Graphs
mnemonics.content: K5 and K3,3 are NOT planar - Think 'K' for 'Kritical' (cannot be drawn)

mnemonics.title: K5 vs K3,3
mnemonics.content:
K5 = 5 vertices, all connected (pentagon with all diagonals)
K3,3 = 3+3 bipartite, like two teams playing each other

acronyms.usage: Formula relating vertices, edges, and faces in planar graphs. Remember: n for nodes, m for edges, f for faces.
Non-planar - every pair of vertices connected, requires 5 complete connections.

acronyms.expansion: Complete bipartite graph with 3 vertices in each part
acronyms.usage: Non-planar - like two sets of 3 people where everyone in one set knows everyone in the other.
Used in graph traversal and finding shortest paths in unweighted graphs.
Used for exploring graph structure and detecting cycles.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a polyhedron (like a cube) unfolded flat. Count vertices (corners), edges (lines), and faces (regions) - the formula always balances to 2.
Think of a road map - if you can draw all roads without overpasses, it's planar. K5 needs a 3D structure (pyramid) to avoid crossings.
Color each region including the outer infinite region as a different 'country' - each country is a face.
Draw a face in the original graph, put a dot in its center - connect dots if faces share an edge. Like country capitals and borders.

analogies.analogy: Like a subway map drawn on flat paper - all stations (vertices) and tracks (edges) visible without any line crossing another.
Imagine 3 boys and 3 girls at a party - if every boy dances with every girl, you can't draw connections without some crossing.
Countries on a map: Each country is a face, borders are edges, and capitals (dual vertices) connect to neighboring countries' capitals.
Two different-looking jigsaw puzzles with the same number of pieces that connect in exactly the same way - they're isomorphic.
Walking around a city block - the number of streets you walk on equals the face's degree. Each street counted once if you walk around a single block.
